title: 001 — A Radial Decision Boundary for a Toy Classification Model

31 May 2026
A placeholder file for derivations and notes on the radial-boundary toy classification problem.

title: 001 — A Radial Decision Boundary for a Toy Classification Model
text:
Questions

What is the probability that a random point \(X=(X_1,X_2)\), distributed according to a distribution \(P_X\) centered at the origin, lies within the Euclidean ball \[ \mathcal B_2(0,r) = \{x\in\mathbb R^2:\|x\|_2\le r\} \] in the following cases?


\(P_X\) is the uniform distribution on the square \([-a,a]^2\), with independent coordinates, for some \(a>r\ge 0\).
\(P_X\) is the bivariate normal distribution \(\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I_2)\), for some \(\sigma>0\).


Consider the following binary classification model. Let \(f(X)=\mathbf 1_{\{X\in \mathcal B_2(0,r)\}}.\) The observed label \(Y\) is defined by \[ Y = \begin{cases}
   f(X), & \text{with probability } 1-\eta,\\
   1-f(X), & \text{with probability } \eta.
   \end{cases} \]


What is the probability that \(Y=1\) in this model for the cases considered in Question 1?

title: 001 — A Radial Decision Boundary for a Toy Classification Model
text:
Derivation

1. Probability of the ball event
The following two cases assume the noiseless setting where the label is the indicator of membership in the ball: \(Y = \mathbf{1}_{X \in \mathcal B_2(0,r)}\).

Uniform distribution on ([-a,a]^2).

Since \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are independent and uniformly distributed on \([-a,a]\), the joint density function of \(X\) is given by \[f_X(x_1,x_2) = \frac{1}{4a^2} \quad \text{for } (x_1,x_2) \in [-a,a]^2\] and \(f_X(x_1,x_2) = 0\) otherwise.The probability that \(X\) lies within the ball \(\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) is given by the integral of the density function over the region defined by the ball:
\[\begin{align*}
\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \int_{\mathcal B_2(0,r)} f_X(x_1,x_2) , dx_1 dx_2 \\\
&= \int_{x_1=-a}^{a} \int_{x_2=-a}^{a} \frac{1}{4a^2} \mathbf{1}_{x_1^2 + x_2^2 \le r^2} , dx_1 dx_2 \\\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^r \frac{1}{4a^2} \rho , d\rho d\theta \\\
&= \frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2}.
\end{align*}\]
Geometric interpretation: The probability \(\mathbb{P}(Y=1)\) can be interpreted as the ratio of the area of the circle defined by the ball \(\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) to the area of the square \([-a,a]^2\). The area of the circle is \(\pi r^2\) and the area of the square is \(4a^2\), hence the probability is \(\frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2}\). More precisely,
\[\begin{align*}\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \frac{\lambda(\mathcal B_2(0,r) \cap [-a,a]^2)}{\lambda([-a,a]^2)} \\\
&= \frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2},\end{align*}\] where \(\lambda\) denotes the Lebesgue measure on \(\mathbb{R}^2\).

Bivariate normal distribution \(\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I_2)\).

Let \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I_2)\). The probability that \(X\) lies within the ball \(\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) is given by \[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = \mathbb{P}(\|X\|_2 \le r) = \mathbb{P}\left(\frac{\|X\|_2^2}{\sigma^2} \le \frac{r^2}{\sigma^2}\right).\]
Since \(\frac{\|X\|_2^2}{\sigma^2} = \frac{X_1^2 + X_2^2}{\sigma^2}\) follows a chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom, we have
\[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = F_{\chi^2_2}\left(\frac{r^2}{\sigma^2}\right),\]
where \(F_{\chi^2_2}\) is the cumulative distribution function of the chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom.
The other calculation approach is the same as in case 1, but with the density function of the bivariate normal distribution: \[f_X(x_1,x_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{x_1^2 + x_2^2}{2\sigma^2}\right).\] The probability can be computed as \[\begin{align*}\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \int_{\mathcal B_2(0,r)} f_X(x_1,x_2) , dx_1 dx_2 \\\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^r \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\rho^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \rho , d\rho d\theta \\\
&= 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right).\end{align*}\]


2. Noisy model — label noise \(\eta\)
The following assumes the noisy label model described in Question 2: with probability \(1-\eta\) the label equals \(f(X)=\mathbf{1}_{X\in\mathcal B_2(0,r)}\), and with probability \(\eta\) it is flipped.

Recall the noiseless (true) label is \(Y_0=1\) when \(X\in\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) and \(Y_0=0\) otherwise. In the noisy case, the observed label equals the true label with probability \((1-\eta)\) and is flipped with probability \(\eta\). Hence \[p(x) := \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X=x) = (1-\eta)f(x) + \eta (1-f(x)).\]
Integrating over \(X\) gives

\[\begin{aligned}\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X=x) f_X(x) , dx \\\
&= (1-\eta)\int f(x)f_X(x) ,dx + \eta\int (1-f(x))f_X(x) ,dx.
\end{aligned}\]
Let \(q=\mathbb{P}(X\in\mathcal B_2(0,r))\). Then the above simplifies to \[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = (1-\eta) q + \eta (1-q) = \eta + (1-2\eta)q.\]
For the uniform case \(q=\pi r^2/(4a^2)\), so
\[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = \eta + (1-2\eta)\frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2}.\]
For the Gaussian case \(q=1-\exp(-r^2/(2\sigma^2))\), so \[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = \eta + (1-2\eta)\left(1- e^{-r^2/(2\sigma^2)}\right).\]
